narrative:
Johnny Olszewski Jr. — universally branded "Johnny O" in a state where nicknames are practically a credential — arrived in Congress in January 2025 with a resume that reads like a Democratic strategist's wish list. First-generation college graduate. Former public school teacher. Ph.D. in public policy from UMBC. Nine years in the Maryland House of Delegates, where he became the youngest lawmaker ever to chair Baltimore County's House delegation [1]. Two terms as Baltimore County Executive, managing a $5 billion budget and nine thousand employees. He cast a pivotal vote for marriage equality, championed earned sick leave, and helped push through a minimum wage increase [2]. On paper, he is exactly the kind of policy-credentialed, working-class-roots Democrat that the 80% Bill was designed for.

The County Executive years burnished his brand but also revealed cracks in the transparency facade. Olszewski created Baltimore County's first Office of Inspector General and its first public campaign finance system — reforms he still touts on his congressional website [3]. What he mentions less often is what happened next. In 2021, after Inspector General Kelly Madigan published reports critical of a county employee and Olszewski fundraiser named Chris McCollum — who later pleaded guilty to embezzlement — Olszewski's office drafted a bill to strip the IG of independence and investigative power [4]. The Association of Inspectors General issued an open letter expressing "grave concerns," warning the legislation would create "the mere appearance of oversight, which is much worse than no oversight at all" [5]. Olszewski shelved the bill only after the Baltimore Brew disclosed its existence, triggering public backlash. He then avoided strengthening the IG's powers again in 2023 [6]. The man who created the watchdog also tried to muzzle it when it barked at the wrong person.

In Congress, Olszewski landed on the Small Business and Foreign Affairs committees and secured a seat on the Democratic Steering and Policy Committee — not bad for a freshman [7]. His voting record shows a near-perfect attendance rate, missing just one of 449 roll call votes through early 2026, and he voted with the Trump administration's position only 13.6 percent of the time [8]. He has introduced legislation on community college access and firearms dealer security requirements, issues consistent with his county-level record. His early congressional tenure is competent, cautious, and largely uncontroversial.

The 80% Bill question for Olszewski is whether the reformer brand survives contact with Washington's incentive structures. His Baltimore County record shows a politician who genuinely instituted transparency mechanisms — and then tried to kneecap them when they became inconvenient. Congress offers exponentially more opportunities to talk reform while quietly protecting the status quo. Olszewski has the policy chops and the populist biography to champion every plank of the 80% Bill. The IG episode suggests he might also be the type to support an Audit the Pentagon bill while making sure the auditors cannot actually subpoena anything uncomfortable.

Robert F. Onder, Jr. is a physician, attorney, and freshman Republican congressman representing Missouri's 3rd Congressional District. A founding member of the Missouri Senate's Conservative Caucus, Onder built his political brand on filibustering everything from needle exchanges to Medicaid financing, earning perfect scores from the American Conservative Union and the National Federation of Independent Businesses [1][2]. He won his 2024 congressional race after pumping $500,000 of his own money into the contest and securing endorsements from both Donald Trump and the Club for Growth [3][4]. In other words, he arrived in Washington pre-certified as a man who will fight government spending with every fiber of his being — unless that spending benefits the people who funded his rise.

On the 80% Bill, Onder's record is a masterclass in selective populism. He has pledged support for Congressional Term Limits, which is easy when you just got there [5]. He has made no move to support a Ban on Congressional Stock Trading, a Lifetime Lobbying Ban, or the DISCLOSE Act — reforms that would inconvenience the very donor class that bankrolled his campaign [6]. On Medicare Drug Negotiation, Onder's Club for Growth backers have long opposed letting the government negotiate prescription prices, and Onder has dutifully offered no support for the policy [7]. He has shown no interest in Banning Corporate PACs, Auditing the Pentagon, or ending Dark Money — the financial architecture that helped build his career. On Paid Family Leave and Banning Junk Fees, the self-described free-market champion sees government mandates as anathema, never mind that 80% of Americans support both [8].

Onder notably filibustered Missouri's congressional redistricting effort in 2022, not to make maps fairer, but to ensure they maximized Republican advantage — the precise opposite of Fair Elections and End Gerrymandering [9][10]. He has been silent on the Right to Repair, Protecting US Farmland from foreign buyers, and Banning Corporate Purchase of Single Family Homes, issues where his free-market orthodoxy conveniently aligns with corporate interests. On Funding Social Security, Onder's Club for Growth philosophy views entitlement reform as code for cuts, not expansion [11]. He declined to take the Vote Smart Political Courage Test, leaving voters to guess where he stands on most issues that matter to them [12].

Follow the money and the silence makes sense. The Club for Growth — funded by hedge fund managers and private equity titans — spent heavily to put Onder in office [3][4]. His top industry contributors include real estate, health professionals, and securities and investment firms [13]. These are precisely the sectors that benefit when Congress fails to close tax loopholes, tax the ultra-wealthy, or ban corporate PACs. Onder self-funded half a million dollars of his own campaign, which means he is literally invested in keeping the system that made him wealthy enough to buy his own seat.

The bottom line: Bob Onder represents the 80% Bill's nightmare scenario — a politician who wraps himself in populist rhetoric about fighting the establishment while opposing virtually every policy that 80% of Americans actually want. He will filibuster government spending on healthcare, but not the dark money pipeline that put him in Congress. He will champion term limits in theory while building the donor relationships that make incumbency permanent in practice. Missouri's 3rd District sent a self-funding physician-lawyer-politician to Washington and got exactly what the Club for Growth ordered.

Frank Pallone Jr. has represented New Jersey's 6th district since 1988 — a tenure so long that when he first took office, the Berlin Wall was still standing and 'streaming' meant a creek behind your house. He is now serving his nineteenth full term, making him one of the longest-serving members of the New Jersey delegation and a living argument for the term limits he has never cosponsored [1].

Pallone's power center is the House Energy and Commerce Committee, where he served as Chairman from 2019 to 2022 and now sits as Ranking Member [2]. The committee's jurisdiction reads like a lobbyist's wish list: health care, pharmaceuticals, energy, environment, consumer protection, and telecommunications. Pallone has used the gavel to push consumer-friendly legislation — the Secure and Trusted Communications Networks Act, the Stopping Bad Robocalls Act, and the HEARTS Act of 2024 [3]. He also championed the Protecting Americans' Data from Foreign Adversaries Act.

But the gavel attracts its own kind of gravity. Over his career, Pallone has accepted roughly $2.2 million from the pharmaceutical and health products industry, according to OpenSecrets data [4]. In a June 2025 House hearing, HHS Secretary Robert F. Kennedy Jr. accused Pallone of taking '$2 million from pharmaceutical companies, more than any member of this committee' — a claim Kennedy was later asked to retract by the committee chair, though not before the clip went viral [5][6]. The irony is that in 1997, Pallone himself had pushed the FDA to investigate mercury in drugs and foods, earning a reputation as a vaccine-safety hawk [7]. Critics say the donations and the policy evolution track a little too neatly.

His top contributing industries over the years include health professionals, pharmaceuticals, telephone utilities, and telecom services — essentially the sectors his committee regulates [4]. He also ranks among top congressional recipients of contributions from registered lobbyists [8]. His estimated net worth as of 2018 was approximately $3.4 million [9].

On the 80% Bill's twenty articles, Pallone has supported Medicare drug-negotiation expansion and data-privacy reform, both squarely in his committee wheelhouse. But he has not championed a lifetime lobbying ban, congressional term limits, or legislation to ban corporate PACs — positions that would, conveniently, inconvenience someone who has spent 38 years converting committee jurisdiction into campaign contributions. When you have been in the House long enough to qualify for a historical plaque, reform starts to look less like a platform and more like an exit sign.

Rep. August Pfluger represents Texas's 11th congressional district — home of the Permian Basin, the beating heart of American oil extraction — and he represents it the way a defense attorney represents a client: zealously, unconditionally, and without asking too many questions about what happened. A former F-22 pilot and Trump National Security Council staffer, Pfluger entered Congress in 2021 and immediately filed legislation to block the Biden administration from pausing new drilling permits on federal lands. His first bill. Not veterans' benefits. Not border security. Drilling permits.

The numbers tell the story with brutal clarity. Pfluger is the top recipient of oil and gas campaign contributions in the entire United States Congress — more than Ted Cruz, more than any presidential candidate in 2024. OpenSecrets pegged his oil and gas haul at over $573,000 in a single cycle, with PAC money flowing from the American Petroleum Institute, Chevron, ExxonMobil, and Koch Industries. He sits on the House Energy and Commerce Committee as the first Permian Basin representative to do so, a fact his office trumpets like a championship banner. He also violated the STOCK Act by failing to properly disclose stock trades worth between $10,000 and $150,000 — the kind of "oops" that conveniently benefits people who trade on insider knowledge about energy policy.

On the 80% Bill's twenty policies, Pfluger is a near-perfect zero. He has taken no public position on banning congressional stock trading — ironic, given his STOCK Act violation. He has not supported term limits, the DISCLOSE Act, dark money bans, corporate PAC bans, or any campaign finance reform of any kind. On taxing the ultra-wealthy and closing tax loopholes, he is predictably silent. On Medicare drug negotiation, paid family leave, funding Social Security, banning junk fees, and right to repair, his record is a blank page — or more accurately, a page filled with "no" votes on Democratic-led versions of these broadly popular measures.

On January 6, 2021, Pfluger voted to block certification of Arizona's and Pennsylvania's electoral votes — not exactly the resume of someone committed to fair elections. He has shown no interest in auditing the Pentagon despite his military background, no movement on ending forever wars despite serving on Foreign Affairs, and no support for body cameras or police accountability. His committee assignments — Homeland Security, Foreign Affairs, Energy and Commerce — give him jurisdiction over at least half the 80% Bill's provisions, which makes his inaction a choice, not an oversight.

The 11th district of Texas stretches across 29 counties of oil country, ranching communities, and small towns where people support every single one of these twenty policies by supermajority margins. Pfluger has decided that Chevron's priorities matter more. Until his constituents decide otherwise, he will keep cashing those checks and calling it service.

Alex Padilla is California's senior senator and the first Latino to represent the state in the U.S. Senate, appointed by Governor Gavin Newsom in January 2021 after Kamala Harris vacated the seat. The son of Mexican immigrants — his father a line cook, his mother a house cleaner — Padilla earned a mechanical engineering degree from MIT before entering politics through the Los Angeles City Council, where he became the youngest-ever council president at 26 [1]. He later served as California's Secretary of State before ascending to the Senate, where he now serves as Ranking Member of the Rules and Administration Committee, a perch he uses to champion voting rights and campaign finance reform [2].

On the substance of the 80% Bill, Padilla checks a remarkable number of boxes. He cosponsored the Ban Congressional Stock Trading Act [3], cosponsored the Close the Revolving Door Act to permanently ban former members of Congress from lobbying [4], joined Bernie Sanders in reintroducing Medicare for All [5], and vocally supported the Inflation Reduction Act's Medicare drug price negotiation provisions [6]. He co-led the Freedom to Vote Act — which includes the DISCLOSE Act to unmask dark money donors — and has called Citizens United a "complete disaster" [7][8]. On gerrymandering, he introduced the Redistricting Reform Act to mandate independent redistricting commissions nationwide and ban mid-decade redistricting [9]. He cosponsored the FAMILY Act for comprehensive paid family and medical leave [10], and has repeatedly blasted Republican tax bills as giveaways to the "billionaire class at the expense of the working class" [11].

Yet Padilla's record is not without its silences. He has not championed congressional term limits — understandable, perhaps, for someone who was handed a Senate seat without ever winning a primary for it. His positions on right to repair, junk fee bans, and banning corporate purchases of single-family homes remain largely unspoken, even as his state's housing crisis screams for action. On the Epstein files, he has criticized the Trump administration's refusal to release them but has not pushed standalone legislation to force disclosure [12]. And while he talks a strong game on Pentagon accountability and ending forever wars, he has not emerged as a leader on AUMF repeal or Pentagon audit legislation.

Follow the money and the picture gets more interesting. In the 2019-2024 cycle, Padilla raised heavily from lawyers and law firms — his top industry at $1,487,135 — with PAC contributions making up nearly 20% of his total fundraising [13]. He received substantial contributions from lobbyists during the 2022 cycle [14]. For a senator who calls Citizens United a "complete disaster," accepting nearly a fifth of his campaign funds from PACs requires a certain flexibility of principle. Padilla is not the worst offender in the Senate by any stretch, but the gap between his populist rhetoric on dark money and his own fundraising architecture is the kind of contradiction that makes voters reach for the cynicism.

The bottom line: Padilla is a genuine ally on many of the 80% Bill's core provisions — stock trading bans, lobbying restrictions, Medicare reform, campaign finance transparency, and anti-gerrymandering efforts. His personal story lends authenticity to his populist positioning. But on the issues where powerful donors might object — term limits, corporate housing purchases, Pentagon accountability — his voice gets conspicuously quieter. He is, in the end, a senator who says most of the right things while carefully avoiding the ones that might cost him.

Nancy Pelosi has represented San Francisco in Congress since 1987, serving nearly four decades and two stints as Speaker of the House -- the first woman to hold that gavel, and the most powerful Democrat in Washington for the better part of a generation [1]. She announced her retirement in November 2025, capping a career defined by legislative mastery, relentless fundraising, and an uncanny ability to talk like a reformer while operating the machinery of the status quo with surgical precision [2]. By any measure, she is one of the most consequential legislators in modern American history. She is also, depending on your perspective, a walking argument for several items on the 80% Bill.

On the substance of the 80% Bill, Pelosi's record is genuinely mixed. She championed the For the People Act and its successors, which included provisions to end gerrymandering, ban dark money, and require campaign finance disclosure -- hitting several of the twenty bills at once [3]. She vocally opposed Citizens United, calling it "undemocratic" and pushing for a constitutional amendment to overturn it [4]. She fought to include paid family leave in the Build Back Better framework, personally adding four weeks back into the bill after it was stripped out [5]. She supported the repeal of the 2002 Iraq AUMF, arguing Congress must "reassert its authority" over war powers [6]. And she backed the Inflation Reduction Act, which included the landmark Medicare drug price negotiation provision [7]. On paper, that is an impressive alignment with popular supermajority priorities.

But the gaps are cavernous and impossible to ignore. Start with congressional stock trading: when first asked about banning the practice in 2021, Pelosi's response was a masterclass in quiet-part-loud -- "We are a free-market economy. They should be able to participate in that" [8]. She reversed course in 2022 under withering public pressure, eventually declaring she "strongly" supported a ban, but as Speaker she had blocked multiple stock trading ban bills from reaching the floor for a vote [9]. Meanwhile, her husband Paul Pelosi, a venture capitalist, had been racking up returns that would make a hedge fund blush: a 54% portfolio return in a single year, roughly doubling the S&P 500, with a career average of 14.5% annually [10]. The Pelosis' estimated net worth sits somewhere between $281 million and $413 million depending on the source -- a sum that would take roughly 3,300 years to accumulate on a congressional salary alone [10][11]. Paul Pelosi's trades have shown a striking pattern of buying shares in companies shortly before favorable federal actions, a coincidence that has spawned an entire cottage industry of "Pelosi tracker" investment apps [12]. On term limits, Pelosi served 38 years in the House and 20 years as party leader, agreeing only to a soft limit on her speakership when younger members forced the issue in 2018 [13]. She never championed broader congressional term limits. And while she railed against dark money in speeches, she presided over one of the most prolific fundraising operations in Democratic Party history, raising hundreds of millions through joint fundraising committees and PAC-to-PAC transfers [14].

Follow the money and the picture sharpens. Pelosi's fundraising apparatus has been less a campaign operation than a political institution. The Nancy Pelosi Victory Fund raised over $1.3 million in the 2023-2024 cycle alone, with top donors including entertainment and corporate executives [14]. Over her career, her top contributing sectors have included securities and investment, real estate, and law firms -- industries with direct stakes in tax policy, financial regulation, and the carried interest loophole [15]. She voted for every major defense authorization as Speaker, including a $738 billion military budget in 2019, while the Pentagon has never once passed a clean audit [16]. She talked about auditing the Pentagon the way one talks about flossing -- important in theory, perpetually deferred.

Pelosi's legacy will be debated for decades, but through the lens of the 80% Bill, her story is one of selective reform. She fought hard for the policies that cost her donor base nothing -- democracy reform language, AUMF repeal, paid leave -- while slow-walking or outright obstructing the ones that threatened her family's financial interests and the party's fundraising infrastructure. She is proof that you can be brilliant, effective, and historically significant while still being part of the problem the supermajority wants solved.

Gary Peters has spent a career being the most effective senator nobody outside Michigan can name. Born in Pontiac and raised in metro Detroit, Peters collected degrees the way some politicians collect lobbyist dinners — a bachelor's from Alma College, an MBA from the University of Detroit, a law degree from Wayne State, and later a master's in philosophy from Michigan State, because apparently a man who spent 22 years as an investment advisor and served in the Navy Reserve still felt intellectually underqualified [1]. He joined the Reserve at 34, earned the Seabee Combat Warfare Specialist designation, deployed to the Persian Gulf, and rose to lieutenant commander before leaving in 2008 — a military record that gives his defense policy positions a credibility most of his colleagues can only approximate through flag lapel pins [2].

Peters entered Congress in 2009 representing Michigan's 14th District, where he promptly became one of the auto industry bailout's most vocal champions — a position that, in a state where the Big Three are practically a fourth branch of government, was both courageous and mathematically obvious [3]. He won his Senate seat in 2014 as the only non-incumbent Democrat to flip a Senate race that cycle, a feat that should have earned him more national attention but instead earned him more committee assignments. As chairman and ranking member of the Homeland Security and Governmental Affairs Committee, he has focused on cybersecurity, government accountability, and border security — topics that rarely generate cable news segments but occasionally prevent actual catastrophes [4].

His bipartisan credentials are not performative. The Lugar Center ranked Peters the most bipartisan Senate Democrat in 2023, and the Center for Effective Lawmaking named him one of the most effective senators in the 116th Congress — a distinction he achieved while in the minority party, something the Center had not recorded in over fifty years of data [5]. He chaired the Democratic Senatorial Campaign Committee for the 2022 and 2024 cycles, a thankless fundraising role that requires a politician to spend most of their time asking rich people for money on behalf of other politicians, which Peters executed with characteristic quiet competence [6].

In January 2025, Peters announced he would not seek reelection in 2026, citing a desire to spend time with his family — including a new grandchild on the West Coast — and to pass the torch to a younger generation [7]. His retirement leaves a conspicuous gap in the Senate's pragmatist caucus. Peters was one of twelve Senate Democrats who voted for the Laken Riley Act in 2025, a move that drew progressive ire but reflected his longstanding willingness to cross party lines when he calculated the policy merited it [8]. Whether history remembers him as a quiet institutionalist or simply the senator who proved you could be both effective and invisible remains to be seen, but his legislative record suggests the former designation is the more accurate one.

Chellie Pingree is that uncommon figure in Congress: a former organic farmer and ex-CEO of Common Cause who somehow ended up as one of the wealthiest members of the House. Representing Maine's 1st Congressional District since 2009, the 70-year-old Democrat came to Washington after running the nation's oldest nonpartisan government accountability organization, where she championed campaign finance reform and lobbied against the very influence she would later need to navigate [1]. She sits on both the House Appropriations and Agriculture committees, and her biography reads like a progression from 1970s back-to-the-land idealism to 21st-century legislative pragmatism — with a detour through a billionaire's hedge fund portfolio along the way [2].

On the 80% Bill issues, Pingree's record is strong where it counts. She helped draft the Fair Elections Now Act, which would create a voluntary public financing system for congressional campaigns, and has cosponsored the Government By the People Act to incentivize small-dollar donations [3]. End Citizens United endorsed her for re-election, citing her commitment to getting corporate money out of politics [4]. On agriculture, she has been one of the House's most persistent advocates for organic farming standards and farmland protection, securing wins for local and organic farming provisions in successive farm bills [5]. She supports strengthening Social Security and has vowed to fight any effort to cut Medicare benefits, positions consistent with her broader progressive voting record on labor rights, consumer protection, and the social safety net [6]. She backed the Justice in Policing Act, which included body camera mandates and police accountability measures [7].

Where the narrative gets interesting is the money. After marrying hedge fund billionaire S. Donald Sussman in 2011, Pingree's net worth skyrocketed, making her the 12th richest member of Congress at an estimated $31.5 million [8]. Though the couple divorced in 2016, Pingree continued to receive up to $1 million annually through a business partnership with Paloma Partners, Sussman's hedge fund, with her share of the fund valued at up to $5 million [9]. For a former Common Cause president who built her career railing against the influence of big money in politics, the optics of drawing hedge fund income while voting on financial regulation are, to put it gently, rich. Her 2024 campaign funding tells a similar story: about half of her contributions came from PACs, with large individual donations making up another 37 percent — not exactly the small-dollar revolution she once championed [10].

Pingree has largely avoided the issues where she might have to choose between her progressive base and her personal financial interests. She has not been a vocal leader on closing the carried-interest loophole — the very tax provision that benefits hedge fund managers like her ex-husband — and her position on taxing the ultra-wealthy remains safely within Democratic orthodoxy without pushing the envelope. More recently, she has made headlines for building an unlikely bridge to MAHA movement mothers on food and agriculture policy, a move that suggests political savvy even if it raises eyebrows among her traditional allies [11].

The bottom line: Chellie Pingree has the resume, the committee seats, and the voting record of a genuine campaign finance reformer and progressive champion. But the hedge fund income, the PAC-heavy fundraising, and the conspicuous silence on carried-interest taxation make her a case study in the distance between who a politician was before Congress and who they become once inside it.

Senator Rand Paul of Kentucky is the rare creature in Washington who has built an entire brand on principled libertarian dissent -- and then, just often enough, undermines it in ways that make you wonder if the principles have a stock ticker attached. An ophthalmologist by training and the son of former congressman Ron Paul, the younger Paul has served in the Senate since 2011 and has made a career out of being the lone "no" vote, filibustering drone policy on the Senate floor, and generally positioning himself as the last honest man in a den of thieves [1]. It is a compelling act, and at least partially sincere.

On several of the 80% Bill issues, Paul is genuinely ahead of the pack. He co-sponsored the End Endless Wars Act to repeal the 2001 Authorization for Use of Military Force, and in 2026 he was the only Republican to vote in favor of a resolution limiting presidential war powers in Iran [2]. He has co-sponsored a constitutional amendment for congressional term limits and signed the U.S. Term Limits pledge [3]. He joined Bernie Sanders and Chuck Grassley to introduce the Audit the Pentagon Act, which would force the Department of Defense to pass a full independent audit or return one percent of its budget to the Treasury [4]. He has even proposed banning federal contractors from lobbying the government or contributing to campaigns during the life of their contracts [5]. On paper, this is a populist dream ticket.

The trouble is what happens when libertarian ideology meets popular demand. Paul opposes Medicare drug price negotiation on free-market grounds, despite the policy polling above 80 percent with voters of both parties [6]. He voted against the Inflation Reduction Act, which contained the first-ever Medicare negotiation provisions. He has opposed the DISCLOSE Act and efforts to ban dark money, framing them as violations of free speech -- a position that conveniently aligns with the interests of donors who prefer to stay anonymous [7]. On paid family leave, taxing the ultra-wealthy, and funding Social Security, Paul's libertarian convictions reliably place him opposite the supermajorities who support these policies.

Then there is the matter of his own stock trades. In 2021, it emerged that Paul's wife purchased between $1,001 and $15,000 in Gilead Sciences stock on February 26, 2020 -- just as Congress was being briefed on COVID-19 and days before Paul voted against a coronavirus emergency spending bill. The purchase was disclosed 16 months late, in violation of the STOCK Act, the very law Paul had once championed in 2012 [8]. The Campaign Legal Center filed a formal complaint. Paul called it an "oversight." He then went on to oppose the bipartisan congressional stock trading ban in committee in 2025, arguing the bill was "crummy" because it exempted the president [9].

The bottom line on Rand Paul is a familiar Washington paradox: a man who talks a better game on government accountability than almost anyone in the Senate, yet whose own financial disclosures required a formal ethics complaint to surface. He will audit the Pentagon and end the forever wars -- but do not ask him to disclose his family's stock trades on time or let Medicare negotiate drug prices. The principle, it seems, has its limits.

Scott Perry has built a congressional career on the principle that the federal government cannot be trusted — unless, of course, it is being used to overturn an election he lost faith in. The former chairman of the House Freedom Caucus has spent over a decade in Congress railing against Washington corruption while quietly embodying its most creative forms.

Perry's starring role in the January 6th saga remains his most enduring contribution to public life. He personally introduced Donald Trump to Jeffrey Clark, the mid-level DOJ official willing to help overturn the 2020 election results. Text messages to Mark Meadows revealed Perry frantically counting down the days to January 6th, texting "We gotta get going!" as he pushed to install Clark atop the Justice Department. The FBI seized his cellphone in August 2022 as part of the investigation, uncovering over 2,200 documents. Perry fought the seizure in court, invoking the Constitution's "speech or debate" clause — apparently the founding fathers anticipated that congressmen would need protection while plotting to subvert democracy.

On stock trading, Perry claims to support a ban and has "crossed the partisan aisle for the past decade" to end congressional insider trading, according to his campaign. Yet after fourteen years in office, his opponent noted he has not actually managed to ban anything. His personal financial disclosures show approximately zero in publicly traded assets tracked by watchdog sites — either a model of restraint or a model of creative accounting.

Perry signed the U.S. Term Limits pledge, supporting a constitutional amendment to cap House members at three terms. He is currently serving his seventh term. The pledge, it turns out, was more of a suggestion.

On foreign aid, Perry voted against emergency military assistance to Israel — not out of any anti-war conviction, but because the bill included humanitarian aid for Gaza, which he characterized as "funding for Hamas." AIPAC promptly paused fundraising for his campaign. He also voted against Ukraine aid, preferring what he called "single subject bills" — a convenient procedural objection that allowed him to vote against helping two allies in a single afternoon.

Perry sits on the Foreign Affairs, Oversight, and Intelligence committees, but has shown no interest in the Pentagon audit that the Defense Department has failed every year since 2018. He champions "readiness and lethality" in military spending while opposing any mechanism to track where the money actually goes. For a man who does not trust the government to count ballots, he shows remarkable faith in its ability to spend $886 billion on defense without oversight.

On the DISCLOSE Act, dark money reform, lobbying bans, paid family leave, and Medicare drug negotiation, Perry's record is a masterpiece of silence. The Freedom Caucus brand demands small government — except when big government is seizing phones, overturning elections, or writing blank checks to the Pentagon.
